title: Not having a hometown
summary: A diplomat's child, now thirty-eight, describes what it is actually like to not have a single place to call home.
body:
I grew up in three countries. My father was a diplomat. We moved every three years until I was fifteen, when my parents divorced and I stayed in Madrid with my mother. I am thirty-eight now. I have lived in Madrid for twenty-three years. People sometimes ask me where I am from. I have, over the decades, given six different answers. None of them was a lie. None was complete.

I want to describe what it is like to not have a hometown. This is not an essay about loss. The loss, if there was one, was processed before I was old enough to identify it. What I want to describe is the specific shape of the absence.

The first shape is conversational. When someone tells me about their grandmother's kitchen, or the colour of the bus they took home from school, I can listen for an hour without fatigue. The stories are fascinating. They are also, in a small way, foreign. I have many small versions. None of them resolves into the same single object.

The second shape is geographical. I have, in my memory, the way light falls on a balcony in Lisbon in October, the smell of a bakery in Vienna in winter, a particular tree in a park in Buenos Aires that I climbed at seven. None of these is connected to the others. My memory is a series of postcards from a holiday I did not entirely take.

The third shape is administrative. I have three passports. My answer to 'Where are you from?' depends on whether the person asking is at a border, at a dinner party, or in a tax office. It becomes a problem when a person I love wants me to choose one. The choice is not really available to me.

What I have, instead, is a kind of attention. I notice quickly which language a stranger is using. I do not assume that any street is permanent. I expect things to move. This is useful at work. It is less useful in relationships.

I would not recommend my situation. I would also not recommend trying to undo it. The thing about a missing hometown is that the person who would have lived in it is also missing. There is no foundation under the absence.

annotations.meaningVi: bưu thiếp (nghĩa bóng: mảnh ký ức rời rạc)

grammarNotes.title: 'Would' for the unreal / hypothetical person or life
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng 'would + V' để chỉ một người, một cuộc đời, hay một lựa chọn không có thật. Trong bài: 'I would not recommend my situation.' (giả định khuyên) 'I would also not recommend trying to undo it.' 'The person who would have lived in it is also missing.' (người không tồn tại — kết cấu loại 3). Khi nói về 'không có cái gì đó', 'would have + V3' chỉ ra rằng 'người/việc đáng lẽ có' cũng không có.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 'would + verb' to refer to an unreal person, life, or choice. The essay: 'I would not recommend my situation.' (hypothetical advice) 'I would also not recommend trying to undo it.' 'The person who would have lived in it is also missing.' (a non-existent counterpart — third-conditional form). Talking about an absence often requires this 'would have' to say the missing counterpart was also missing.

translationVi:
Tôi lớn lên ở ba nước. Cha tôi là một nhà ngoại giao. Cứ ba năm chúng tôi chuyển, đến năm tôi mười lăm, khi cha mẹ tôi ly hôn và tôi ở lại Madrid với mẹ. Giờ tôi ba mươi tám. Tôi đã sống ở Madrid hai mươi ba năm. Người ta đôi khi hỏi tôi từ đâu đến. Qua nhiều thập kỷ, tôi đã đưa ra sáu câu trả lời khác nhau. Không cái nào là dối. Không cái nào là đầy đủ.

Tôi muốn mô tả cảm giác không có một quê hương. Đây không phải bài về mất mát. Mất mát, nếu có, đã được xử lý trước khi tôi đủ lớn để nhận ra nó. Tôi muốn mô tả hình dáng cụ thể của sự vắng mặt.

Hình dáng thứ nhất là hội thoại. Khi ai đó kể tôi nghe về căn bếp của bà ngoại họ, hay màu chiếc xe buýt họ đi học về, tôi có thể nghe cả tiếng không mệt. Câu chuyện hấp dẫn. Cũng, theo một nghĩa nhỏ, là xa lạ. Tôi có nhiều phiên bản nhỏ. Không phiên bản nào hợp lại thành cùng một vật duy nhất.

Hình dáng thứ hai là địa lý. Trong ký ức tôi có cách ánh sáng chiếu lên ban công ở Lisbon vào tháng Mười, mùi của một tiệm bánh ở Vienna vào mùa đông, một cây cụ thể trong một công viên ở Buenos Aires mà tôi đã leo khi bảy tuổi. Không cái nào liên kết với cái khác. Ký ức của tôi là một chuỗi bưu thiếp từ một chuyến đi mà tôi chưa hoàn toàn đi.

Hình dáng thứ ba là hành chính. Tôi có ba hộ chiếu. Câu trả lời 'Anh từ đâu đến?' tuỳ vào người hỏi đang ở biên giới, ở bữa tối, hay ở sở thuế. Nó trở thành vấn đề khi một người tôi yêu muốn tôi chọn một cái.

Cái tôi có, thay vào đó, là một dạng chú ý. Tôi để ý nhanh người lạ đang dùng ngôn ngữ nào. Tôi không giả định rằng bất kỳ con phố nào là vĩnh viễn. Tôi mong mọi thứ sẽ chuyển. Có ích trong công việc. Ít có ích hơn trong các mối quan hệ.

Tôi sẽ không khuyên ai vào hoàn cảnh của tôi. Tôi cũng không khuyên cố gắng xoá bỏ nó. Vấn đề với một quê hương vắng mặt là người đáng lẽ đã sống ở đó cũng vắng mặt. Không có nền móng dưới sự vắng mặt.

questions.prompt: How long has the writer lived in Madrid?
questions.explanation: 'I have lived in Madrid for twenty-three years.'

questions.prompt: How many different answers has she given to 'Where are you from?'
What does she say about the essay's topic?
options: It is not about loss; it is about the specific shape of the absence.

questions.prompt: What is the FIRST shape of the absence?
options: Conversational — other people's hometown stories are fascinating but foreign.
Geographical — disconnected memories, like 'postcards from a holiday I did not entirely take.'
Administrative — three passports; the answer depends on whether one is at a border, dinner party, or tax office.

questions.prompt: When does the third shape become a real problem?
options: When someone she loves wants her to choose one.
questions.explanation: 'It becomes a problem when a person I love wants me to choose one.'

questions.prompt: What does she have 'instead' of a hometown?
options: A kind of attention — noticing languages, not assuming streets are permanent.

questions.prompt: Where is this 'attention' useful and where is it less useful?
options: Useful at work; less useful in relationships.
Useful in tests; less useful at home.

questions.prompt: What is her final claim about the missing hometown?
options: The person who would have lived in it is also missing — there is no foundation under the absence.
She is glad she does not.
It is the same as everyone else's.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the essay NOT claiming?
options: That her answer to 'Where are you from?' shifts with context.
That a missing hometown comes with a missing 'would-have-been' self.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the essay?
options: Not having a single hometown produces a specific, three-shaped absence — conversational, geographical, administrative — that cannot be undone because the 'would-have-been' person is missing too.
Madrid is the writer's true home.
Having three passports is a privilege.
questions.explanation: The three named shapes and the final 'no foundation' line.
questions.hint: Notice the three shapes and the closing line.

cloze.template:
I grew up in three {{b1}}. My father was a diplomat. We moved every three years until I was fifteen, when my parents divorced and I stayed in Madrid with my mother. I am thirty-eight now. I have lived in Madrid for twenty-three years. People sometimes ask me where I am from. I have, over the decades, given {{b2}} different answers. None of them was a lie. None was complete.

I want to describe what it is like to not have a {{b3}}. This is not an essay about loss. The loss, if there was one, was processed before I was old enough to identify it. What I want to describe is the specific shape of the {{b4}}.

The first shape is conversational. When someone tells me about their grandmother's kitchen, or the colour of the bus they took home from school, I can listen for an hour without {{b5}}. The stories are fascinating. They are also, in a small way, foreign. I have many small versions. None of them resolves into the same single {{b6}}.

The second shape is geographical. I have, in my memory, the way light falls on a balcony in Lisbon in October, the smell of a bakery in Vienna in winter, a particular tree in a park in Buenos Aires that I climbed at seven. None of these is connected to the others. My memory is a series of {{b7}} from a holiday I did not entirely take.

The third shape is administrative. I have three {{b8}}. My answer to 'Where are you from?' depends on whether the person asking is at a border, at a dinner party, or in a tax office. It becomes a problem when a person I love wants me to choose one. The choice is not really available to me.

What I have, instead, is a kind of {{b9}}. I notice quickly which language a stranger is using. I do not assume that any street is permanent. I expect things to move. This is useful at work. It is less useful in {{b10}}.

I would not recommend my situation. I would also not recommend trying to undo it. The thing about a missing hometown is that the person who would have lived in it is also missing. There is no {{b11}} under the absence.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says the person who would have lived in a hometown is also missing. Is there a 'would-have-been' version of yourself — a self who took the other job, married the other person, stayed in the home town — that you imagine sometimes? What does that imagined person quietly cost you?
